- [tc24r](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r) -- Tiny COR24 compiler in Rust
- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) -- COR24 assembler and emulator

# Architecture: Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c)

## System Overview

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │              tml24c Runtime                   │
                    │                                              │
  UART input ──────►  Reader ──► S-expression (Value tree)        │
                    │                    │                          │
                    │                    ▼                          │
                    │              Macro Expand                     │
                    │                    │                          │
                    │                    ▼                          │
                    │               Evaluator ◄──── Environment    │
                    │                    │            (lexical)     │
                    │                    ▼                          │
                    │               Printer ────────► UART output  │
                    │                                              │
                    │          ┌─── Heap ───┐                      │
                    │          │ cons pairs  │                      │
                    │          │ symbols     │◄──── GC (mark-sweep) │
                    │          │ strings     │                      │
                    │          │ closures    │                      │
                    │          │ macros      │                      │
                    │          └─────────────┘                      │
                    │                                              │
                    │         Platform Abstraction Layer            │
                    └──────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────┘
                               │                       │
                    ┌──────────▼──────┐     ┌─────────▼──────────┐
                    │  Host (gcc)     │     │  COR24 (tc24r)     │
                    │  stdio putchar  │     │  UART MMIO         │
                    │  malloc arena   │     │  SRAM heap         │
                    └─────────────────┘     └────────────────────┘
```

## Components

### Reader (`read.c`)
Converts text input into Value trees (S-expressions).

**Tokens:** `(`, `)`, `'`, `` ` ``, `,`, `,@`, `"string"`, integers, symbols.

**Produces:** Tagged Value objects — fixnums (immediate), symbols and lists (heap-allocated cons pairs).

### Evaluator (`eval.c`)
Tree-walking interpreter. Dispatches on value type and special form tag.

**Special forms:**

# Detailed Design: Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c)

## Data Representation

### Tagged Values

All Lisp values are represented as a single 24-bit word (`typedef int Value;` on COR24, `typedef int32_t Value;` on host).

```
Bit layout (24 bits):
[23 ..................... 2][1][0]
         payload            tag
```

| Tag | Type | Payload |
|-----|------|---------|
| 00 | Fixnum | 22-bit signed integer (arithmetic shift right 2 to decode) |
| 01 | Cons | Heap index (cell number, not byte address) |
| 10 | Symbol | Heap index |
| 11 | Extended | Heap index → object with runtime type header |

**Fixnum encoding:**
```c
#define MAKE_FIXNUM(n)   (((n) << 2) | TAG_FIXNUM)
#define FIXNUM_VAL(v)    ((v) >> 2)           // arithmetic shift preserves sign
#define IS_FIXNUM(v)     (((v) & 3) == TAG_FIXNUM)
```

**Pointer encoding:**
```c
#define MAKE_CONS(idx)   (((idx) << 2) | TAG_CONS)
#define CONS_IDX(v)      ((v) >> 2)
#define IS_CONS(v)       (((v) & 3) == TAG_CONS)
```

### Special Values

```c
#define NIL_VAL          MAKE_SYMBOL(0)   // symbol index 0 = nil
#define T_VAL            MAKE_SYMBOL(1)   // symbol index 1 = t
#define IS_NIL(v)        ((v) == NIL_VAL)
#define IS_TRUE(v)       (!IS_NIL(v))     // everything non-nil is true
```

### Fixnum Range

22-bit signed: -2,097,152 to 2,097,151. Sufficient for demos, loop counters, list indices. Overflow wraps silently (no bignum fallback).

# tc24r -- Tiny C Compiler for the COR24 FPGA Soft CPU

tc24r is an open-source C compiler targeting the **COR24** (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit) instruction set architecture. COR24 is an FPGA soft CPU designed by [MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) for efficient C code execution. The existing MakerLisp C compiler (cc24) is proprietary -- tc24r provides an open-source alternative.

The approach is inspired by [chibicc](https://github.com/rui314/chibicc), a small educational C compiler. tc24r follows the same architecture pattern (recursive-descent parser, direct assembly emission) but is written from scratch in Rust.

## Project Status

tc24r is **functional** -- it compiles real C programs to COR24 assembly that runs on hardware and the cor24-rs emulator. 14 components, ~50 crates, 46 demos all passing. See [docs/status.md](docs/status.md) for detailed status and test counts.

### What Works

- Types: int (24-bit), unsigned int, char (8-bit), void, pointers, arrays
- All standard C operators with correct precedence
- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case
- Ternary operator (? :), character literals, multi-declaration
- Prefix/postfix increment and decrement (++i, i--)
- Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=
- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct (dot and arrow access), union
- Function prototypes (forward declarations, mutual recursion)
- Functions with multiple parameters, recursion, ISR support
- Globals, string constants, hex literals
- Pointer arithmetic with element-size scaling
- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
- Inline assembly: asm("...")
- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt enable
- Software division and modulo runtime

### What Does Not Work Yet

- Multi-file compilation
- float/double (COR24 has no FPU -- out of scope)

## COR24 Architecture at a Glance

- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
- **8 registers**: r0-r2 (general purpose), fp, sp, z (zero), iv, ir
- **32 instructions**: arithmetic, logic, shift, compare, branch, load/store, stack ops
- **1/2/4-byte** variable-length instruction encoding
- Hardware multiply (24-cycle), no hardware divide
- Little-endian, byte-addressable memory
- UART and GPIO via memory-mapped I/O

## Component Structure

The compiler is organized into 14 components with ~50 crates, each component
being its own Cargo workspace under `components/`:

| Component | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| core | Shared types: AST, tokens, spans, errors, traits |
| frontend | Preprocessing, lexing, parsing |
| backend | Legacy codegen and validation |
| codegen-emit | Assembly emission (core, data, load/store) |
| codegen-expr | Expression codegen (call, literal, ops, pointer, variable) |
| codegen-ops | Operator codegen (arithmetic, bitwise, compare, divmod, incdec, logical, unary, type-infer) |
| codegen-state | Codegen state management |
| codegen-stmt | Statement codegen (control flow, simple statements) |
| codegen-structure | Function structure (ISR, locals, prologue) |
| config | Configuration and target definition |
| dispatch | Codegen dispatch layer |
| macros | Proc macros (asm-dsl, emit-macros, handler-macros) |
| testing | Test harnesses (as24, compile, cor24, golden) |
| cli | CLI binary entry point |

See [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) for data flow and design constraints.

## Building

Build all components:

```bash
scripts/build-all.sh
```

This runs each component's `scripts/build.sh` in dependency order. The release binary is produced at:

```
components/cli/target/release/tc24r
```

To build a single component:

```bash
cargo build --manifest-path components/<name>/Cargo.toml
```

## Usage

```bash
tc24r <input.c> [-o output.s] [-I dir]
```

- `<input.c>` -- C source file to compile
- `-o output.s` -- output assembly file (default: stdout)
- `-I dir` -- add include search path (repeatable)

# MakerLisp COR24 — Assembly Emulator

A browser-based educational emulator for the
[MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) COR24 (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit)
architecture. Written in Rust and compiled to WebAssembly.

**[Live Demo](https://sw-embed.github.io/cor24-rs/)**

### Assembler Tab
![COR24 Assembler Tab](images/assembler-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)

### C Tab
![COR24 C Pipeline Tab](images/c-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)

### Rust Tab
![COR24 Rust Pipeline Tab](images/rust-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)

### CLI Pipeline Demos

![Assembler UART Hello](docs/vhs/asm-uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)

![Rust Pipeline UART Hello](docs/vhs/uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)

## Features

- **Interactive Assembly Editor** — Write and edit COR24 assembly code
- **Step-by-Step Execution** — Debug your code instruction by instruction
- **Multi-Region Memory Viewer** — Program, Stack, and I/O regions with change heatmaps
- **CLI Debugger** (`cor24-dbg`) — GDB-like command-line debugger with breakpoints, UART, and LED/button I/O
- **LGO File Loader** — Load programs assembled with the reference `as24` toolchain
- **Built-in Examples** — Learn from pre-loaded example programs
- **Challenges** — Test your assembly skills with programming challenges
- **ISA Reference** — Instruction set documentation and memory map

## COR24 Architecture

COR24 is a 24-bit RISC soft CPU for Lattice MachXO FPGAs, designed for
embedded systems education. 32 operations encode into 211 instruction
forms (1, 2, or 4 bytes).

- **3 General-Purpose Registers**: r0, r1, r2 (24-bit)
- **5 Special-Purpose Registers**:
  - r3 = fp (frame pointer)
  - r4 = sp (stack pointer, init 0xFEEC00)
  - r5 = z (always zero; usable only in compare instructions)
  - r6 = iv (interrupt vector)
  - r7 = ir (interrupt return address)
- **Single Condition Flag**: C (set by compare instructions)
- **16 MB Address Space**: 1 MB SRAM + 3 KB EBR (stack) + memory-mapped I/O
- **Variable-Length Instructions**: 1, 2, or 4 bytes

## Summary: tml24c Project Architecture and Step 008 Compiler Meaning

### **Source Files Overview**

The tml24c project in `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c/src/` contains these components:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **tml.h** | Core type definitions: tagged value encoding (Fixnum, Cons, Symbol, Extended), all values are 24-bit with 2-bit tags |
| **io.h** | UART I/O primitives for COR24-TB platform (MMIO-based character I/O) |
| **heap.h** | Heap management: parallel arrays (heap_car, heap_cdr), bump allocator, cons cell creation |
| **symbol.h** | Symbol table: string interning, maintaining unique symbol identifiers |
| **print.h** | Value printer: outputs fixnums, symbols, lists, extended objects to UART |
| **read.h** | S-expression reader: parses integers, symbols, lists, dotted pairs, and quote syntax from input strings |
| **eval.h** | Tree-walking evaluator: special forms (quote, if, define, lambda, defmacro, begin), environment management, primitive operations, closure and macro handling |
| **gc.h** | Mark-sweep garbage collector: protects roots, marks reachable cells from global environment and explicit root stack, sweeps unmarked cells |
| **main.c** | Test suite: scaffolding tests, reader tests, evaluator tests (arithmetic, conditionals, lambdas, closures, recursion), GC tests |

### **Current Main.c - Tests and Functionality**

The executable is a **test suite**, not an REPL. It runs in this sequence:

1. **test_scaffold()** — Basic value construction (fixnums, symbols, cons pairs, lists)
2. **test_reader()** — Parser validation (integers, symbols, lists, dotted pairs, quote)
3. **test_eval()** — Evaluator validation covering:

1→# COR24 ISA Reference
     2→
     3→C-Oriented RISC 24-bit Instruction Set Architecture
     4→
     5→## Overview
     6→
     7→COR24 is a 24-bit RISC architecture designed for efficient C code execution. It features:
     8→
     9→- **24-bit data path** - Registers and addresses are 24 bits wide
    10→- **3 general-purpose registers** - r0, r1, r2
    11→- **5 special-purpose registers** - fp, sp, z, iv, ir
    12→- **Single condition flag** - C (carry/condition)
    13→- **Variable-length instructions** - 1, 2, or 4 bytes
    14→- **16MB address space** - Full 24-bit addressing
    15→- **Little-endian** byte ordering
    16→- **Memory-mapped I/O**
    17→
    18→## Registers
    19→
    20→| Register | Name | Description |
    21→|----------|------|-------------|
    22→| r0 | - | General purpose / return value |
    23→| r1 | - | Return address (link register) |
    24→| r2 | - | General purpose |
    25→| r3 | fp | Frame pointer |
    26→| r4 | sp | Stack pointer |
    27→| r5 | z | Zero (for compare instructions) |
    28→| r6 | - | Interrupt vector |
    29→| r7 | - | Used for absolute jumps |
    30→| PC | - | Program counter (24-bit) |
    31→| C | - | Condition flag (1-bit) |
    32→
    33→## Memory Map
    34→
    35→| Address Range | Description |
    36→|---------------|-------------|
    37→| 0x000000 - 0xFDFFFF | SRAM (external memory) |
    38→| 0xFE0000 - 0xFEFFFF | Embedded block RAM (fast) |
    39→| 0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF | Memory-mapped I/O |
    40→
    41→The CPU starts execution at address 0xFEE000 on reset.
    42→
    43→## Instruction Set Summary
    44→
    45→### Arithmetic Instructions
    46→
    47→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    48→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    49→| `add ra,rb` | 1 | 0x00 | ra = ra + rb |
    50→| `add ra,dd` | 2 | 0x01 | ra = ra + sign_extend(dd) |
    51→| `sub ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1A | ra = ra - rb |
    52→| `sub sp,dddddd` | 4 | 0x1B | sp = sp - dddddd (24-bit) |
    53→| `mul ra,rb` | 1 | 0x12 | ra = ra * rb (lower 24 bits) |
    54→
    55→### Logical Instructions
    56→
    57→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    58→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    59→| `and ra,rb` | 1 | 0x02 | ra = ra & rb |
    60→| `or ra,rb` | 1 | 0x13 | ra = ra \| rb |
    61→| `xor ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1E | ra = ra ^ rb |
    62→
    63→### Shift Instructions
    64→
    65→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    66→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    67→| `shl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x17 | ra = ra << rb[4:0] |
    68→| `sra ra,rb` | 1 | 0x18 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (arithmetic) |
    69→| `srl ra,rb` | 1 | 0x19 | ra = ra >> rb[4:0] (logical) |
    70→
    71→### Compare Instructions
    72→
    73→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    74→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    75→| `ceq ra,rb` | 1 | 0x06 | C = (ra == rb) |
    76→| `cls ra,rb` | 1 | 0x07 | C = (ra < rb) signed |
    77→| `clu ra,rb` | 1 | 0x08 | C = (ra < rb) unsigned |
    78→
    79→### Branch Instructions
    80→
    81→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    82→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    83→| `bra dd` | 2 | 0x03 | PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
    84→| `brf dd` | 2 | 0x04 | if (!C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
    85→| `brt dd` | 2 | 0x05 | if (C) PC = PC + sign_extend(dd) |
    86→
    87→### Jump Instructions
    88→
    89→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    90→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    91→| `jmp (ra)` | 1 | 0x0A | PC = ra |
    92→| `jal ra,(rb)` | 1 | 0x09 | ra = PC + 1; PC = rb |
    93→
    94→### Load Instructions
    95→
    96→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
    97→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
    98→| `la ra,dddddd` | 4 | 0x0B | ra = dddddd (24-bit address) |
    99→| `lb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0C | ra = sign_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
   100→| `lbu ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x0D | ra = zero_extend(mem[rb + dd]) |
   101→| `lc ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0E | ra = sign_extend(dd) |
   102→| `lcu ra,dd` | 2 | 0x0F | ra = zero_extend(dd) |
   103→| `lw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x10 | ra = mem24[rb + dd] |
   104→
   105→### Store Instructions
   106→
   107→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
   108→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
   109→| `sb ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x16 | mem[rb + dd] = ra[7:0] |
   110→| `sw ra,dd(rb)` | 2 | 0x1C | mem24[rb + dd] = ra |
   111→
   112→### Move Instructions
   113→
   114→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
   115→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
   116→| `mov ra,rb` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = rb |
   117→| `mov ra,c` | 1 | 0x11 | ra = C (when rb=5) |
   118→| `sxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1D | ra = sign_extend(rb[7:0]) |
   119→| `zxt ra,rb` | 1 | 0x1F | ra = zero_extend(rb[7:0]) |
   120→
   121→### Stack Instructions
   122→
   123→| Mnemonic | Bytes | Opcode | Description |
   124→|----------|-------|--------|-------------|
   125→| `push ra` | 1 | 0x15 | sp -= 3; mem24[sp] = ra |
   126→| `pop ra` | 1 | 0x14 | ra = mem24[sp]; sp += 3 |
   127→
   128→## Instruction Encoding
   129→
   130→Instructions are encoded with a lookup ROM that maps 8-bit opcodes to decoded operations. The instruction byte encodes the operation and register operands together.
   131→
   132→### Single-Byte Instructions (Register Operations)
   133→
   134→| Byte | Instruction |
   135→|------|-------------|
   136→| 0x01 | add r0,r1 |
   137→| 0x02 | add r0,r2 |
   138→| 0x1A | cls r0,r2 |
   139→| 0x1B | cls r1,r0 |
   140→| 0x25 | jal r1,(r0) |
   141→| 0x27 | jmp (r1) |
   142→| 0x57 | mov r0,r2 |
   143→| 0x65 | mov fp,sp |
   144→| 0x69 | mov sp,fp |
   145→| 0x7A | pop r1 |
   146→| 0x7B | pop r2 |
   147→| 0x7C | pop fp |
   148→| 0x7D | push r0 |
   149→| 0x7E | push r1 |
   150→| 0x7F | push r2 |
   151→| 0x80 | push fp |
   152→| 0xCE | clu z,r0 |
   153→
   154→### Two-Byte Instructions (Register + Immediate)
   155→
   156→| First Byte | Instruction Format |
   157→|------------|-------------------|
   158→| 0x09 | add r0,imm8 |
   159→| 0x0B | add r2,imm8 |
   160→| 0x0C | add sp,imm8 |
   161→| 0x13 | bra offset8 |
   162→| 0x14 | brf offset8 |
   163→| 0x15 | brt offset8 |
   164→| 0x2C | lb r0,(r0) with offset |
   165→| 0x2E | lb r0,(r2) with offset |
   166→| 0x44 | lc r0,imm8 |
   167→| 0x45 | lc r1,imm8 |
   168→| 0x46 | lc r2,imm8 |
   169→| 0x4D | lw r0,offset(fp) |
   170→| 0x51 | lw r1,offset(fp) |
   171→| 0x55 | lw r2,offset(fp) |
   172→| 0x82 | sb r0,(r2) with offset |
   173→| 0x84 | sb r1,(r0) with offset |
   174→| 0xA6 | sw r0,offset(fp) |
   175→
   176→### Four-Byte Instructions (Register + 24-bit Address)
   177→
   178→| First Byte | Instruction Format |
   179→|------------|-------------------|
   180→| 0x29 | la r0,addr24 |
   181→| 0x2A | la r1,addr24 |
   182→| 0x2B | la r2,addr24 |
   183→
   184→## Calling Convention
   185→
   186→### Function Prologue
   187→```asm
   188→push    fp          ; Save frame pointer
   189→push    r2          ; Save callee-saved register
   190→push    r1          ; Save return address
   191→mov     fp,sp       ; Set up frame pointer
   192→add     sp,-N       ; Allocate stack space
   193→```
   194→
   195→### Function Epilogue
   196→```asm
   197→mov     sp,fp       ; Restore stack pointer
   198→pop     r1          ; Restore return address
   199→pop     r2          ; Restore callee-saved register
   200→pop     fp          ; Restore frame pointer
   201→jmp     (r1)        ; Return
   202→```
   203→
   204→### Argument Passing
   205→- Arguments are pushed right-to-left onto the stack
   206→- Return value is in r0
   207→- Caller cleans up arguments with `add sp,N`
   208→
   209→### Stack Frame Layout
   210→```
   211→Higher addresses
   212→+----------------+
   213→| Argument N     | fp + (N*3 + 6)
   214→| ...            |
   215→| Argument 1     | fp + 9
   216→+----------------+
   217→| Saved r1       | fp + 6
   218→| Saved r2       | fp + 3
   219→| Saved fp       | fp + 0
   220→+----------------+
   221→| Local var 1    | fp - 3
   222→| Local var 2    | fp - 6
   223→| ...            |
   224→+----------------+
   225→Lower addresses (sp)
   226→```
   227→
   228→## Word Size
   229→
   230→- Words are 24 bits (3 bytes)
   231→- Stack operations push/pop 3 bytes at a time
   232→- Word addresses should be 3-byte aligned for efficiency
   233→
   234→## Example: Fibonacci
   235→
   236→```asm
   237→_fib:
   238→        push    fp              ; Save frame pointer
   239→        push    r2              ; Save r2
   240→        push    r1              ; Save return address
   241→        mov     fp,sp           ; Set up frame
   242→        add     sp,-3           ; Local variable space
   243→        lw      r2,9(fp)        ; Load argument n
   244→
   245→        lc      r0,2            ; Load constant 2
   246→        cls     r2,r0           ; Compare n < 2
   247→        brf     L17             ; Branch if false
   248→
   249→        lc      r0,1            ; Return 1
   250→        bra     L16             ; Jump to epilogue
   251→
   252→L17:
   253→        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
   254→        add     r0,-1           ; r0 = n - 1
   255→        push    r0              ; Push argument
   256→        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
   257→        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-1)
   258→        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
   259→        sw      r0,-3(fp)       ; Save result
   260→
   261→        mov     r0,r2           ; r0 = n
   262→        add     r0,-2           ; r0 = n - 2
   263→        push    r0              ; Push argument
   264→        la      r0,_fib         ; Load fib address
   265→        jal     r1,(r0)         ; Call fib(n-2)
   266→        add     sp,3            ; Clean up argument
   267→        lw      r1,-3(fp)       ; Load fib(n-1)
   268→        add     r0,r1           ; r0 = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
   269→
   270→L16:
   271→        mov     sp,fp           ; Restore stack
   272→        pop     r1              ; Restore return address
   273→        pop     r2              ; Restore r2
   274→        pop     fp              ; Restore frame pointer
   275→        jmp     (r1)            ; Return
   276→```
   277→
   278→## Interrupts
   279→
   280→- Interrupt vector is in r6
   281→- When an interrupt occurs, `jal r7,(r6)` is executed
   282→- Return from interrupt with `jmp (r7)`
   283→- Interrupts can be disabled by the `intis` flag
   284→
   285→## References
   286→

   288→- Example programs: `demo/*.lst`
   289→- Hardware manual: `doc/COR24-TB-MAN.pdf`
   290→- MakerLisp: https://makerlisp.com

1→//! COR24 register definitions.
     2→
     3→/// Register names indexed by 3-bit register number.
     4→pub const REG_NAMES: [&str; 8] = ["r0", "r1", "r2", "fp", "sp", "z", "r6", "r7"];
     5→
     6→/// Get register name from 3-bit index.
     7→pub fn reg_name(reg: u8) -> &'static str {
     8→    REG_NAMES[(reg & 0x07) as usize]
     9→}
    10→
    11→/// Parse a register name to its 3-bit index.
    12→pub fn parse_register(s: &str) -> Option<u8> {
    13→    match s.to_lowercase().as_str() {
    14→        "r0" => Some(0),
    15→        "r1" => Some(1),
    16→        "r2" => Some(2),
    17→        "fp" => Some(3),
    18→        "sp" => Some(4),
    19→        "z" | "c" => Some(5),
    20→        "iv" => Some(6),
    21→        "ir" => Some(7),
    22→        _ => None,
    23→    }
    24→}
    25→

1→//! COR24 instruction opcodes and formats.
     2→
     3→/// COR24 instruction opcodes (5-bit, from decoded ROM)
     4→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
     5→#[cfg_attr(feature = "serde", derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize))]
     6→#[repr(u8)]
     7→pub enum Opcode {
     8→    AddReg = 0x00, // add ra,rb
     9→    AddImm = 0x01, // add ra,dd
    10→    And = 0x02,    // and ra,rb
    11→    Bra = 0x03,    // bra dd
    12→    Brf = 0x04,    // brf dd
    13→    Brt = 0x05,    // brt dd
    14→    Ceq = 0x06,    // ceq ra,rb
    15→    Cls = 0x07,    // cls ra,rb (compare less signed)
    16→    Clu = 0x08,    // clu ra,rb (compare less unsigned)
    17→    Jal = 0x09,    // jal ra,(rb)
    18→    Jmp = 0x0A,    // jmp (ra)
    19→    La = 0x0B,     // la ra,dddddd
    20→    Lb = 0x0C,     // lb ra,dd(rb) (signed)
    21→    Lbu = 0x0D,    // lbu ra,dd(rb) (unsigned)
    22→    Lc = 0x0E,     // lc ra,dd (signed)
    23→    Lcu = 0x0F,    // lcu ra,dd (unsigned)
    24→    Lw = 0x10,     // lw ra,dd(rb)
    25→    Mov = 0x11,    // mov ra,rb or mov ra,c
    26→    Mul = 0x12,    // mul ra,rb
    27→    Or = 0x13,     // or ra,rb
    28→    Pop = 0x14,    // pop ra
    29→    Push = 0x15,   // push ra
    30→    Sb = 0x16,     // sb ra,dd(rb)
    31→    Shl = 0x17,    // shl ra,rb
    32→    Sra = 0x18,    // sra ra,rb
    33→    Srl = 0x19,    // srl ra,rb
    34→    Sub = 0x1A,    // sub ra,rb
    35→    SubSp = 0x1B,  // sub sp,dddddd
    36→    Sw = 0x1C,     // sw ra,dd(rb)
    37→    Sxt = 0x1D,    // sxt ra,rb
    38→    Xor = 0x1E,    // xor ra,rb
    39→    Zxt = 0x1F,    // zxt ra,rb
    40→    Invalid = 0xFF,
    41→}
    42→
    43→impl From<u8> for Opcode {
    44→    fn from(value: u8) -> Self {
    45→        match value {
    46→            0x00 => Opcode::AddReg,
    47→            0x01 => Opcode::AddImm,
    48→            0x02 => Opcode::And,
    49→            0x03 => Opcode::Bra,
    50→            0x04 => Opcode::Brf,
    51→            0x05 => Opcode::Brt,
    52→            0x06 => Opcode::Ceq,
    53→            0x07 => Opcode::Cls,
    54→            0x08 => Opcode::Clu,
    55→            0x09 => Opcode::Jal,
    56→            0x0A => Opcode::Jmp,
    57→            0x0B => Opcode::La,
    58→            0x0C => Opcode::Lb,
    59→            0x0D => Opcode::Lbu,
    60→            0x0E => Opcode::Lc,
    61→            0x0F => Opcode::Lcu,
    62→            0x10 => Opcode::Lw,
    63→            0x11 => Opcode::Mov,
    64→            0x12 => Opcode::Mul,
    65→            0x13 => Opcode::Or,
    66→            0x14 => Opcode::Pop,
    67→            0x15 => Opcode::Push,
    68→            0x16 => Opcode::Sb,
    69→            0x17 => Opcode::Shl,
    70→            0x18 => Opcode::Sra,
    71→            0x19 => Opcode::Srl,
    72→            0x1A => Opcode::Sub,
    73→            0x1B => Opcode::SubSp,
    74→            0x1C => Opcode::Sw,
    75→            0x1D => Opcode::Sxt,
    76→            0x1E => Opcode::Xor,
    77→            0x1F => Opcode::Zxt,
    78→            _ => Opcode::Invalid,
    79→        }
    80→    }
    81→}
    82→
    83→/// Decoded instruction with opcode and register operands
    84→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
    85→pub struct DecodedInstruction {
    86→    pub opcode: Opcode,
    87→    pub ra: u8,
    88→    pub rb: u8,
    89→}
    90→
    91→impl DecodedInstruction {
    92→    pub fn new(opcode: Opcode, ra: u8, rb: u8) -> Self {
    93→        Self {
    94→            opcode,
    95→            ra: ra & 0x07,
    96→            rb: rb & 0x07,
    97→        }
    98→    }
    99→
   100→    /// Decode from 12-bit ROM output
   101→    pub fn from_decoded(decoded: u16) -> Self {
   102→        let opcode = Opcode::from(((decoded >> 6) & 0x1F) as u8);
   103→        let ra = ((decoded >> 3) & 0x07) as u8;
   104→        let rb = (decoded & 0x07) as u8;
   105→        Self { opcode, ra, rb }
   106→    }
   107→}
   108→
   109→/// Instruction format type
   110→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
   111→pub enum InstructionFormat {
   112→    /// Single byte: opcode + registers encoded
   113→    SingleByte,
   114→    /// Two bytes: first byte + 8-bit immediate/offset
   115→    TwoBytes,
   116→    /// Four bytes: first byte + 24-bit address
   117→    FourBytes,
   118→}
   119→
   120→impl InstructionFormat {
   121→    /// Size in bytes
   122→    pub fn size(&self) -> usize {
   123→        match self {
   124→            InstructionFormat::SingleByte => 1,
   125→            InstructionFormat::TwoBytes => 2,
   126→            InstructionFormat::FourBytes => 4,
   127→        }
   128→    }
   129→}
   130→
   131→impl Opcode {
   132→    /// Get the instruction format for this opcode
   133→    pub fn format(&self) -> InstructionFormat {
   134→        match self {
   135→            Opcode::AddReg | Opcode::And | Opcode::Ceq | Opcode::Cls | Opcode::Clu
   136→            | Opcode::Jal | Opcode::Jmp | Opcode::Mov | Opcode::Mul | Opcode::Or
   137→            | Opcode::Pop | Opcode::Push | Opcode::Shl | Opcode::Sra | Opcode::Srl
   138→            | Opcode::Sub | Opcode::Sxt | Opcode::Xor | Opcode::Zxt => {
   139→                InstructionFormat::SingleByte
   140→            }
   141→            Opcode::AddImm | Opcode::Bra | Opcode::Brf | Opcode::Brt | Opcode::Lb
   142→            | Opcode::Lbu | Opcode::Lc | Opcode::Lcu | Opcode::Lw | Opcode::Sb
   143→            | Opcode::Sw => InstructionFormat::TwoBytes,
   144→            Opcode::La | Opcode::SubSp => InstructionFormat::FourBytes,
   145→            Opcode::Invalid => InstructionFormat::SingleByte,
   146→        }
   147→    }
   148→
   149→    /// Get mnemonic string for this opcode
   150→    pub fn mnemonic(&self) -> &'static str {
   151→        match self {
   152→            Opcode::AddReg | Opcode::AddImm => "add",
   153→            Opcode::And => "and",
   154→            Opcode::Bra => "bra",
   155→            Opcode::Brf => "brf",
   156→            Opcode::Brt => "brt",
   157→            Opcode::Ceq => "ceq",
   158→            Opcode::Cls => "cls",
   159→            Opcode::Clu => "clu",
   160→            Opcode::Jal => "jal",
   161→            Opcode::Jmp => "jmp",
   162→            Opcode::La => "la",
   163→            Opcode::Lb => "lb",
   164→            Opcode::Lbu => "lbu",
   165→            Opcode::Lc => "lc",
   166→            Opcode::Lcu => "lcu",
   167→            Opcode::Lw => "lw",
   168→            Opcode::Mov => "mov",
   169→            Opcode::Mul => "mul",
   170→            Opcode::Or => "or",
   171→            Opcode::Pop => "pop",
   172→            Opcode::Push => "push",
   173→            Opcode::Sb => "sb",
   174→            Opcode::Shl => "shl",
   175→            Opcode::Sra => "sra",
   176→            Opcode::Srl => "srl",
   177→            Opcode::Sub | Opcode::SubSp => "sub",
   178→            Opcode::Sw => "sw",
   179→            Opcode::Sxt => "sxt",
   180→            Opcode::Xor => "xor",
   181→            Opcode::Zxt => "zxt",
   182→            Opcode::Invalid => "???",
   183→        }
   184→    }
   185→}

1→//! COR24 branch range constants and helpers.
     2→//!
     3→//! Branch instructions (bra, brt, brf) use a 2-byte format:
     4→//!   byte 0: opcode
     5→//!   byte 1: signed 8-bit offset
     6→//!
     7→//! The offset is relative to branch_base = instruction_address + 4
     8→//! (accounting for the COR24 pipeline: 3 prefetch + 1 execute).
     9→
    10→/// Minimum branch offset (signed 8-bit).
    11→pub const BRANCH_OFFSET_MIN: i32 = -128;
    12→
    13→/// Maximum branch offset (signed 8-bit).
    14→pub const BRANCH_OFFSET_MAX: i32 = 127;
    15→
    16→/// Pipeline delay: branch base = instruction_address + this value.
    17→pub const BRANCH_PIPELINE_DELAY: u32 = 4;
    18→
    19→/// Maximum bytes a single instruction can occupy.
    20→pub const MAX_INSTRUCTION_BYTES: usize = 4;
    21→
    22→/// Maximum number of instructions between a branch and its target
    23→/// that guarantees a short branch is safe.
    24→///
    25→/// Calculated as: BRANCH_OFFSET_MAX / MAX_INSTRUCTION_BYTES = 127 / 4 = 31.
    26→/// This is conservative — most instructions are 1-2 bytes, so the actual
    27→/// reachable instruction count is usually higher.
    28→pub const MAX_SHORT_BRANCH_INSTRUCTIONS: usize = 31;
    29→
    30→/// Check if a short branch can reach from one instruction position to another.
    31→///
    32→/// Uses a conservative estimate: each instruction is at most 4 bytes.
    33→/// Returns true if the distance is guaranteed to be within range.
    34→pub fn can_short_branch(from_count: usize, to_count: usize) -> bool {
    35→    let distance = if from_count > to_count {
    36→        from_count - to_count
    37→    } else {
    38→        to_count - from_count
    39→    };
    40→    distance <= MAX_SHORT_BRANCH_INSTRUCTIONS
    41→}

1→# COR24 Assembler (as24) Notes
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→The `asld24` toolchain provides a COR24 assembler (`cor24-as`), linker (`cor24-ld`), and a
     6→load-command compactor (`longlgo`). These are C programs built from source in
     7→`docs/research/asld24/`. We do not have access to the COR24 C compiler; our goal is to
     8→generate correct COR24 assembly from Rust.
     9→
    10→## Building the tools
    11→
    12→```bash
    13→cd docs/research/asld24
    14→make
    15→```
    16→
    17→This produces three binaries: `cor24-as`, `cor24-ld`, and `longlgo`.
    18→
    19→## Usage
    20→
    21→The assembler reads from **stdin** (not a filename argument).
    22→
    23→### Flags
    24→
    25→| Flag | Description |
    26→|------|-------------|
    27→| `-l` | Listing output (assemble only, no link) |
    28→| `-S` | Assembly output |
    29→| `-c` | Assemble only (object output) |
    30→| `-O` | Optimize |
    31→| `-a` | Additional option |
    32→
    33→### Assemble with listing (no link)
    34→
    35→```bash
    36→./cor24-as -l < fib.s
    37→./cor24-as -l < sieve.s
    38→```
    39→
    40→This prints addresses, hex opcodes, and the disassembled instructions. Useful for
    41→verifying that our Rust-generated assembly encodes correctly.
    42→
    43→### Assemble and link
    44→
    45→```bash
    46→./cor24-as < sieve.s
    47→```
    48→
    49→This runs the assembler followed by the linker, producing COR24 monitor load/go
    50→commands on stdout. Only works for self-contained programs (no unresolved externals).
    51→
    52→### Assemble, link, and compact
    53→
    54→```bash
    55→./cor24-as < sieve.s | ./longlgo
    56→```
    57→
    58→Consolidates load commands into longer lines for efficient transfer to the monitor.
    59→
    60→## Example files
    61→
    62→### fib.s
    63→
    64→Fibonacci program compiled from C. Computes `fib(33)` and prints the result.
    65→
    66→- **Links?** No -- references `_printf`, which requires a C library we don't have.
    67→- **Assemble only:** `./cor24-as -l < fib.s`
    68→
    69→Key patterns visible in the listing:
    70→
    71→- Function prologue: `push fp` / `push r2` / `push r1` / `mov fp,sp` / `add sp,-N`
    72→- Function epilogue: `mov sp,fp` / `pop r1` / `pop r2` / `pop fp` / `jmp (r1)`
    73→- Function call: `push` args, `la r0,<func>`, `jal r1,(r0)`, `add sp,N`
    74→- Recursive calls to `_fib` with `r2` as the preserved argument
    75→
    76→### sieve.s
    77→
    78→Sieve of Eratosthenes (1000 iterations, primes up to 8190). Compiled from C.
    79→
    80→- **Links?** Yes -- self-contained with built-in I/O (`_putchr` talks directly to UART).
    81→- **Assemble and link:** `./cor24-as < sieve.s | ./longlgo`
    82→- **Assemble only:** `./cor24-as -l < sieve.s`
    83→
    84→Notable details:
    85→
    86→- `_putchr` does direct UART I/O at address `-65280` (0xFF0100), polling status register
    87→- `_printn` does decimal conversion using a `_divdec` table (1000, 100, 10)
    88→- `_putstr` walks a null-terminated string byte by byte
    89→- `.comm _flags,8191` allocates the sieve array in BSS
    90→
    91→## COR24 architecture quick reference
    92→
    93→- **Address space:** 24-bit (16 MB)
    94→- **GP registers:** r0, r1, r2 (only 3 truly general-purpose)
    95→- **Special registers:** fp (r3), sp (r4), z (r5, always zero, compare-only), r6 (iv), r7 (ir)
    96→- **Instruction sizes:** 1, 2, or 4 bytes (never 3)
    97→- **Branch displacement:** PC-relative; PC is 2 ahead of the branch instruction at execute time
    98→- **Stack:** grows downward; `push` decrements sp by 3 (word size), `pop` increments by 3
    99→- **Word size:** 3 bytes (24 bits)
   100→- **UART:** at 0xFF0100 (data) / 0xFF0101 (status)
   101→- **Silent overflow:** wraps at 24 bits
   102→
   103→## Using as24 to validate Rust output
   104→
   105→To test assembly generated by our Rust pipeline:
   106→
   107→1. Write the `.s` file with COR24 assembly syntax
   108→2. Run `./cor24-as -l < yourfile.s` and inspect the listing
   109→3. Compare hex opcodes against expected encodings
   110→4. For self-contained programs, link with `./cor24-as < yourfile.s | ./longlgo` and
   111→   load onto the FPGA via the monitor
   112→

## COR24 Assembly Language and ISA - Comprehensive Reference

Based on my thorough analysis of the COR24 resources, here's everything you need to understand the COR24 assembly language and emit valid code:

### **1. REGISTER ARCHITECTURE**

**General-Purpose Registers (3 total):**
- `r0` - General purpose / return value / multiply result
- `r1` - Return address (link register)
- `r2` - General purpose / callee-saved

**Special Registers:**
- `r3` (`fp`) - Frame pointer (callee-saved)
- `r4` (`sp`) - Stack pointer
- `r5` (`z`) - Zero register (read-only, always 0, for compare instructions)
- `r6` (`iv`) - Interrupt vector
- `r7` (`ir`) - Interrupt return (used with jmp)
- `PC` - Program counter (24-bit)
- `C` - Condition flag (1-bit, set by compare/arithmetic instructions)

**Key constraint:** Only 3 general-purpose registers. R0, R1, R2 are used for nearly all operations. R3 (fp) and R4 (sp) are special. R5 (z) is read-only.

---

### **2. MEMORY ARCHITECTURE**

**Address Space:** 24-bit (16 MB total)
- `0x000000 - 0xFDFFFF`: SRAM (external memory)
- `0xFE0000 - 0xFEFFFF`: Embedded block RAM (fast)
- `0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF`: Memory-mapped I/O
- CPU starts execution at `0xFEE000` on reset

**Word Size:** 3 bytes (24 bits)
- Stack operations always work with 3-byte words
- `push` decrements sp by 3; `pop` increments by 3
- Addresses should be 3-byte aligned for efficiency

**Byte Ordering:** Little-endian

---

### **3. INSTRUCTION SET SUMMARY**

All instructions are variable-length:
- **1 byte:** Register-register operations (most common)
- **2 bytes:** Register + 8-bit immediate/offset
- **4 bytes:** Register + 24-bit address

#### **Arithmetic Instructions (1 byte)**
```
add ra,rb       ra = ra + rb
sub ra,rb       ra = ra - rb
mul ra,rb       ra = ra * rb (lower 24 bits)
```

#### **Arithmetic with Immediates (2 bytes)**
```
add ra,dd       ra = ra + sign_extend(dd)      [dd = signed 8-bit]
sub sp,dddddd   sp = sp - dddddd               [24-bit immediate]

file.content:
#pragma once

/* tml.h -- Tiny Macro Lisp core types and macros
 *
 * All values are a single COR24 int (24-bit).
 * Low 2 bits = tag, upper bits = payload.
 *
 *   [23..2] payload  [1:0] tag
 *   00  Fixnum   -- 22-bit signed integer
 *   01  Cons     -- heap cell index
 *   10  Symbol   -- symbol table index
 *   11  Extended -- heap cell index (closure, macro, primitive, string)
 */

/* Tags */
#define TAG_FIXNUM   0
#define TAG_CONS     1
#define TAG_SYMBOL   2
#define TAG_EXTENDED 3
#define TAG_MASK     3

/* Tag checks */
#define IS_FIXNUM(v)   (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_FIXNUM)
#define IS_CONS(v)     (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_CONS)
#define IS_SYMBOL(v)   (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_SYMBOL)
#define IS_EXTENDED(v) (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_EXTENDED)

/* Fixnum encode/decode */
#define MAKE_FIXNUM(n)  (((n) << 2) | TAG_FIXNUM)
#define FIXNUM_VAL(v)   ((v) >> 2)

/* Pointer (index) encode/decode */
#define MAKE_CONS(idx)     (((idx) << 2) | TAG_CONS)
#define MAKE_SYMBOL(idx)   (((idx) << 2) | TAG_SYMBOL)
#define MAKE_EXTENDED(idx) (((idx) << 2) | TAG_EXTENDED)
#define PTR_IDX(v)         ((v) >> 2)

/* Special values: nil = symbol 0, t = symbol 1 */
#define NIL_VAL   MAKE_SYMBOL(0)
#define T_VAL     MAKE_SYMBOL(1)
#define IS_NIL(v) ((v) == NIL_VAL)

#define HEAP_SIZE 4096

/* Extended object type tags */
#define ETYPE_CLOSURE   MAKE_FIXNUM(1)
#define ETYPE_MACRO     MAKE_FIXNUM(2)
#define ETYPE_PRIMITIVE MAKE_FIXNUM(3)
#define ETYPE_STRING    MAKE_FIXNUM(4)

TC24R = tc24r
COR24_RUN = cor24-run
SRC = src/main.c
ASM = build/tml24c.s

all: $(ASM)

$(ASM): $(SRC) src/tml.h src/io.h src/heap.h src/symbol.h src/print.h src/read.h src/eval.h src/gc.h
	@mkdir -p build
	$(TC24R) $(SRC) -o $(ASM)

run: $(ASM)
	$(COR24_RUN) --run $(ASM)

clean:
	rm -rf build

content.text:
The compiler works. Let me verify the generated assembly is correct:

**`_cmain`**: Stores `_CL0` (fact) at `_G38`, stores `_CL1` (double) at `_G32`, then computes `(+ 1 2)`: loads 8 (MAKE_FIXNUM(2)), pushes, loads 4 (MAKE_FIXNUM(1)), pops r1, adds → r0 = 12 = MAKE_FIXNUM(3). Correct.

**`_CL0` (fact)**: Tests `(= n 0)` — loads 0, compares with n at fp+9. If equal, returns T_VAL. The `if` then checks if result is nil — if not nil (equality succeeded), returns 4 (MAKE_FIXNUM(1)). Otherwise computes `(* n (fact (- n 1)))` — subtracts 4 (FIXNUM(1)) from n, calls fact recursively via global, multiplies. Correct.

**`_CL1` (double)**: Loads 8 (MAKE_FIXNUM(2)), pushes, loads n from fp+9, pops r1, untags r1 (sra by 2), multiplies. Correct.

Now let me also verify the `fact` function's `if` more carefully — I notice the `(= n 0)` test returns T_VAL/NIL_VAL, and the `if` compares against NIL_VAL(2). The `brf _CL2` jumps to the then-branch (return 1) when NOT nil. That's correct.

But wait — the then-branch at `_CL2` has `lc r0,4` (MAKE_FIXNUM(1)), but so does `_CL3`. Let me re-read: `_CL2` is the then-label in the `if`, and `_CL3` is the else-label. The then case returns `1` = MAKE_FIXNUM(1) = 4. The else case computes `(* n (fact (- n 1)))`. That's correct.

Now let me restore all tests and update the Makefile's run target to use higher speed.
